texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Natural gas is a mixture of hydrocarbons with CH4 as the main species, typically constituting 75 -90% of the raw gas [1,2]. Puri fi ed natural gas is of great importance not only as a source of energy with reduced emissions of CO2 upon combustion as compared to coal and oil but also as a raw material for the chemical industry. It may be classi fi ed as ' wet ' or ' dry ' natural gas depending on if it is associated with crude oil (wet) or found in reservoirs in which no oil is present (dry). Dry natural gas only contains small amount of condensable hydrocarbons, e.g. n -C4H10 < 1% and C3H8 < 2% at ambient temperature, whereas in wet natural gas, the amount of n -C4H10 and C3H8 could reach 11% and 20%, respectively, at ambient temperature [3]. There are many reasons for removing the heavier hydrocarbons from natural gas e.g. to reduce soot and coke formation if the gas is used as fuel or to avoid problems with condensing liquids if the gas is to be compressed and transported in pipelines. Typical U.S. natural gas pipeline speci fi cations are a C3+ content corresponding to between 950 and 1050 Btu/scf (British thermal unit/standard cubic foot) and a dew point lower than -20 °C

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The removal of propane and heavier hydrocarbons (C3+) from natural gas is an important part of natural gas upgrading. In the present work, ultra-thin MFI zeolite membranes with a thickness of 400 nm and an estimated Si/Al ratio of 152 were evaluated for separation of C3H8 and n -C4H10 from binary and ternary mixtures with CH4. The membranes were selective towards the heavier hydrocarbons and showed high permeance at all investigated temperatures. At room temperature, the n -C4H10/CH4 separation selectivity was 25, coupled with an n -C4H10 permeance of 31 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 for a 10/90 n -C4H10/CH4 binary feed mixture. As the temperature was decreased to 281 K, the separation selectivity increased to as high as 55 with an n -C4H10 permeance of 25 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 . The separation selectivities for a 10/90 C3H8/CH4 binary mixture were 9.5 and 19, with C3H8 permeances as high as 54 and 37 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 297 and 271 K, respectively. The higher selectivities observed for n -C4H10 containing mixtures was ascribed to stronger adsorption of n -C4H10 than C3H8 in MFI, thus resulting in higher adsorption selectivities of the n -C4H10 containing mixtures over CH4. For a 10/10/80 n -C4H10/C3H8/CH4 ternary mixture, the highest sum selectivity of ( n -C4H10 +C3H8)/CH4 was 48 and the corresponding sum permance of ( n -C4H10 + C3H8) was 26 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 283 K, which were similar to the separation results of n -C4H10/CH4 binary mixture at the same conditions. The n -C4H10/ CH4 and C3H8/CH4 separation selectivities from the ternary mixture were of course lower, but still as high as 32 and 16 at 283 K, with n -C4H10 and C3H8 permeances of 17 and 8 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , respectively. The results show that ultra-thin MFI zeolite membranes are promising candidates for separation of C3+ hydrocarbons from natural gas.

texts.orig: C4H10/CH4 mixture, a separation selectivity of 30 and a n -C4H10 permeance of as high as 6 × 10 -7 mol s -1 m -2 Pa -1 at room temperature and 16 bar pressure di ff erence across the membrane has been reported [5]. Because of its outstanding mixed-gas separation properties, PTMSP was previously considered to be a promising membrane material for C3+/methane separation. However, PTMSP has low solvent resistance and is consequently soluble in a wide range of organic solvents. Furthermore, the permeability of PTMSP to gases and vapours decreases over time as a consequence of physical aging, and this polymer membrane can therefore not be used in industrial applications [6,7]. The commercially used polymeric membranes e.g. polysulfone, PDMS etc. have much better physical aging resistance, but display separation selectivities of only 0.6 -7, and a C3+ permeance lower than 0.5 × 10 -7 mol s -1 m -2 Pa -1 at room temperature [8]. Low permeance results in large membrane area needed to process a certain gas fl ow, whereas low selectivity necessitate multi-stage processes to minimize CH4 loss, and both of these factors result in an expensive upgrading process. The low solvent stability is also an issue for commercial polymeric membranes and therefore more robust membranes should be developed. Zeolite membranes are considered especially attractive due to the unique properties of zeolites being inorganic crystalline aluminosilicates with well-de fi ned pore size and high chemical and mechanical stability. Zeolite MFI has a pore diameter of ca. 0.55 nm, which is slightly larger than linear hydrocarbons such as n-alkanes. Consequently, MFI membranes have been widely explored for separation of di ff erent gas mixtures of hydrocarbons [9 -12]. Santamaría et al., reported a highest n -C4H10/CH4 selectivity of 31.5 at 25 °C, for silicalite-1 membranes, but the overall permeance was rather low, e.g. the permeance for C2H6 was lower than 1.0 × 10 -7 mol s -1 m -2 Pa -1 [13]. Furthermore, most reports consider relatively thick MFI fi lms, and the reported permeances are thereby low, comparable to polymeric membranes. Since ceramic zeolite membranes will be much more expensive than polymeric membranes per membrane area, zeolite membranes must display much higher permeance than polymeric membranes to be competitive [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ultra-thin (500 nm) MFI zeolite membranes with high permeance have been developed in our group [15]. These membranes have been evaluated during 15 years for many separations, and of particular interest for the present work is our previous work on n/ i-C4H10 separations [15] and ole fi n/nitrogen separations [16]. We have reported an n/ i-C4H10 separation factor of 9 and an n -C4H10 permeance of 9.8 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at room temperature for a 500 nm fi lm [15]. More recently, other groups have also reported very thin MFI membranes. MFI zeolite membranes with the thickness of less than 1 µm have been developed, which are promising advances for their commercial viability [17 -19]. The thinnest membranes have been prepared using nanosheets as seeds, which results in fi lms comprising b-oriented crystals [18]. The membranes had thicknesses of 100 -250 nm and 250 nm ~ 1 µm, displaying a good n-butane/iso-butane selectivity and an extremely high p-xylene/o-xylene separation selectivity, respectively. In the present work, we evaluated our ultra-thin MFI membranes for removal of n -C4H10 and C3H8 from CH4 for the fi rst time. The separations were performed at di ff erent temperatures for binary C3H8/CH4 and n -C4H10/ CH4 mixtures, and also for a n -C4H10/C3H8/CH4 ternary mixture to identify suitable separation conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Porous graded α -alumina discs (Fraunhofer IKTS, Germany) with a diameter of 25 mm were used as supports. The top layer of the support is 30 µm thick with a pore size of 100 nm, and the base layer is 3 mm thick with a pore size of 3 µm. The membranes were prepared as described in a patent application [20]. Brie fl y, a dispersion of 50 nm silicalite-1 crystals was used for obtaining a monolayer of seed crystals on top of the support. After seeding, the MFI zeolite fi lms were grown in a synthesis mixture with a molar composition of 3TPAOH:25SiO2:1450H2O:100EtOH in a plastic tube kept in an oil bath at 88 °C for 55 h under re fl ux. After synthesis, the membranes were rinsed in a 0.1 M NH3 solution overnight and then calcined at 500 °C for 6 h at a heating rate of 0.2 °C min -1 and a cooling rate of 0.3 °C min -1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes were characterized using n -hexane/helium adsorption-branch permporometry [21] in order to estimate the amount of fl ow-through defects. For this purpose, the membranes were mounted in a stainless steel cell sealed with graphite gaskets (Eriks, the Netherlands). A detailed description of the experimental and data evaluation procedures for permporometry is given in previous work [22]. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Magellan 400 fi eld emission XHR-SEM) was used to investigate the morphology of the membranes. The Si/Al ratio of the zeolite fi lm was estimated by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) as descripted in detail in our previous report [23]. We showed that the Cs concentration in Cs-exchanged ZSM-5 crystals is equal to the Al concentration, and consequently, the Al concentration in ZSM-5 zeolite can be accurately estimated by ionexchanging the sample to Cs+ form. The ion exchange was carried out in a 0.1 M Cesium Chloride (p.a., Merck) solution at 100 °C and re fl ux at atmospheric pressure for 1 h. The membranes were thoroughly rinsed with deionized water. The above procedure was repeated three times. The ion-exchanged membrane was coated with carbon before the Cs concentration was measurement by EDS using an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Since Cs is much heavier than Al, the detection limit is improved and, in addition, this method is not in fl uenced by any Al-signal from the alumina support.

texts.orig: 2.2. Separation experiments
texts.text: 2.2. Separation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior to each separation measurement with a new gas mixture, the membranes were dried for 6 h at 300 °C under a fl ow of helium with a heating rate of 1 °C min -1 followed by natural cooling. A WickeKallenbach cell was used and the temperature inside the cell was monitored by a type K thermocouple. Separation experiments commenced when the cell had cooled to 323 K and sub-ambient membrane temperatures were achieved by placing the membrane cell in thermostated silicone oil. The desired feed fl ow rate was achieved by using needle valves and monitoring the fl ow rate by calibrated rotary fl ow meters (Brooks instrument, SHO-RATE, USA). The feed pressure was controlled by a backpressure regulator on the retentate line. The pressure on both sides of the membrane was monitored by pressure gauges. The permeate was kept at atmospheric pressure and no sweep gas was used. The permeate fl ow rate was measured using a drumtype gas meter (Ritter Apparatebau GmbH), and the composition of feed and permeate streams was analysed using a Micro GC (490 Micro GC, Agilent) connected online. A 10/90 n -C4H10/CH4 binary mixture was fed to the membrane at a pressure of 7 bar at a fl ow rate of 5 l min -1 . For separation of the 10/90C3H8/CH4 binary mixture, the mixture was fed to the membrane at a total pressure of 9 bar and a fl ow rate of 10 l min -1 . For separation of the 10/10/90 n -C4H10/C3H8/CH4 ternary mixture, the mixture was fed to the membrane at a total pressure of 9 bar and a fl ow rate of 5 l min -1 . The gas mixtures were purchased from AGA gas company with purities of methane and heavier hydrocarbons of > 99.9995% and > 99.95%, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) equation of state was used for calculating component fugacities at the feed conditions. Gas phase di ff usivities and viscosities of pure components were estimated for the desired temperature using the Di ∝ T 3/2 relationship and Sutherland's equation [24], respectively. The viscosities of mixtures were estimated as molar fraction weighted averages. The ideal solution theory (IAST) [25] was used for estimating adsorbed concentrations for mixture adsorption by using dual site Sips model single component adsorption parameters obtained from literature as input [11,26 -29].
texts.text: The Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) equation of state was used for calculating component fugacities at the feed conditions. Gas phase di ff usivities and viscosities of pure components were estimated for the desired temperature using the Di ∝ T 3/2 relationship and Sutherland's equation [24], respectively. The viscosities of mixtures were estimated as molar fraction weighted averages. The ideal solution theory (IAST) [25] was used for estimating adsorbed concentrations for mixture adsorption by using dual site Sips model single component adsorption parameters obtained from literature as input [11,26 -29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 1 shows top view and cross-sectional SEM images of a calcined MFI membrane. The top view image shows that the fi lm is continuous and appears comprised of well intergrown zeolite crystals at the top surface of the membrane. No defects, such as cracks or pinholes, could be observed in the membrane by SEM. However, grain boundaries can of course be observed between all grains. As we have shown earlier [21], at least some of these grain boundaries are open and have a width of < 2 nm, i.e. being micropores. The cross-sectional image shows that the fi lm appears to be rather even with a total thickness of around 400 nm. Moreover, the support appears completely open and no invasion could be observed. The Si/Al ratio of the membrane was estimated to be 152 by EDS. The small amount of aluminium in the fi lm most likely originates from the support due to leaching during synthesis.

texts.orig: Fig. 4 a shows the adsorbed loadings of C3H8 and CH4 in the same temperature range as the membrane experiments. The adsorbed loading for single component adsorption of C3H8 was high, but lower than saturation (2.04 mol kg -1 ) at all temperatures. For the binary mixture, the adsorbed loading of C3H8 was slightly lower than in the case of single component adsorption as expected due to the some limited competition from CH4 adsorption. For the binary mixture, the amount of C3H8 adsorbed increased with decreasing temperature because of the larger (in absolute value) heat of adsorption for C3H8 than for CH4. The adsorbed loading of CH4, on the other hand, is much lower when adsorbed from the binary mixture as compared to single component adsorption, by ca. a factor 4 -17 depending on the temperature. This shows that C3H8 is signi fi cantly reducing the adsorbed amount of CH4 when adsorbed from the mixture. At the same time, because of the conditions employed in the experiments with relative low temperature and high pressure, the loadings of the heavier hydrocarbon is high. Under these conditions, di ff usional coupling is typically encountered in the MFI framework. This means that the di ff usion of the weaker adsorbing but faster di ff using molecule, CH4 in this case, is slowed down by the stronger adsorbing and less mobile heavier hydrocarbon [26]. However, a desorption controlled mass transport mechanism is also consistent with these observations and this will be investigated. In addition, the measured permeate compositions and the estimated adsorbed loadings on the permeate side are given in the supporting information, Tables S4-S6. The estimated adsorbed loadings on the permeate side is smaller than, but close to the loadings on the feed side of the membrane, especially at low temperature, which indicates that the mass transport may be desorption limited. Anyway, the observed selectivity is dominated by the adsorption selectivity where the heavier component is outcompeting the lighter CH4 molecule in the competitive adsorption. The adsorbed loadings of n -C4H10 and CH4 as a function of temperature are shown in Fig. 4 b . The results are similar to the C3H8/ CH4 system, with the important di ff erence that CH4 adsorption is suppressed much more during competitive adsorption with n -C4H10 than with C3H8. This was expected n -C4H10 adsorbs stronger than C3H8, consequently the observed selectivity for n -C4H10 was signi fi cantly higher than for C3H8. For instance at room temperature, the observed membrane separation selectivity was 25 for the n -C4H10/CH4 binary mixture, which was signi fi cantly higher than the selectivity for the C3H8/CH4 mixture of 9. The permeance of C3H8 was 54 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , which was higher than the permeance of n -C4H10 of 31 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at room temperature. We have previously reported an n -C4H10 permeance of 9.8 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at room temperature for 500 nm fi lms for separation of n/ i-C4H10 isomers [15]. The much higher permeance observed in the present work is attributed to the thinner fi lm, the di ff erent experimental conditions and the weak competitive adsorption of CH4. Moreover, in the present work, the n -C4H10/CH4 selectivity increased from 13 to 55 when the temperature was decreased from 322 K to 281 K, with a corresponding decrease of n -C4H10 permeance from 34 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 to 25 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 for the n -C4H10/CH4 binary mixture, see Fig. 2. The same trend was observed for the C3H8/CH4 binary mixture. When decreasing the temperature from 320 to 271 K, the selectivity increased from 5 to 19 whereas the permeance of C3H8 decreased from 70 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 to 37 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 when the temperature was reduced, see Fig. 3. The observed decrease in permeance with decreasing temperature could be a combined e ff ect of a decrease in di ff usivity or desorption rate with decreasing temperature and perhaps a decreased driving force over the membrane [11,26]. Similar trends have been observed previously for separation of the same or similar gas mixtures by zeolite membranes [12], however the

texts.orig: The separation performance for a 10/10/80 n -C4H10/C3H8/CH4 ternary mixture was also evaluated using the same membrane as for the experiments with the binary mixtures; the obtained separation selectivities and permeances are shown in Figs. 5 and 6.
texts.text: The separation performance for a 10/10/80 n -C4H10/C3H8/CH4 ternary mixture was also evaluated using the same membrane as for the experiments with the binary mixtures; the obtained separation selectivities and permeances are shown in Figs. 5 and 6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane was selective towards the C3 and C4 hydrocarbons in the ternary mixture as well. However, the competitive adsorption between n -C4H10 and C3H8 of course play a role and the observed selectivity for n -C4H10 from the ternary mixture at a given temperature was signi fi cantly lower than the corresponding one for the binary mixture. The adsorbed loading as a function of temperature was estimated by the dual site Sips model and the IAST, see Fig. 7 a and b (see Table S6 in the supporting information for the adsorbed loadings on the permeate side). For ternary mixture (Fig. 7 b ), the n -C4H10/CH4 and C3H8/CH4 adsorption selectivities were high due to the strong adsorption of C3H8/ and n -C4H10 compared to methane. With decreasing temperature, the adsorption of CH4 decreased slightly, and the adsorption selectivity increased. The observed membrane selectivity is therefore dominated by adsorption selectivity as the heavier components are outcompeting the adsorption of the lighter CH4 molecules.

The observed membrane selectivity is therefore dominated by adsorption selectivity as the heavier components are outcompeting the adsorption of the lighter CH4 molecules.

texts.orig: For the ternary mixture at room temperature, the n -C4H10/CH4 separation selectivity was 15 as compared to 25 for the binary mixture. However, the ( n -C4H10 + C3H8)/CH4 separation selectivity was 22 (see Fig. 8) and 48 at 298 and 283 K, respectively, very close to the n -C4H10/ CH4 selectivity in the binary mixtures, which indicates that the n -C4H10 selectivity is reduced only because also C3H8 is permeating and occupying the pores. Fig. 5 shows that the highest observed n -C4H10 permeance was 28 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 308 K and that the permeance decreased with decreasing temperature. The lowest permeance of n -C4H10 was 17 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 283 K, which was lower than the permeance (25 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 281 K) of n -C4H10 observed in the binary mixture separation experiments. However, the sum of the n -C4H10 and C3H8 permeance was 26 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 283 K (see Fig. 8), i.e. very similar to the n -C4H10 permeance from the binary mixture. This indicates that the n -C4H10 permeance is reduced due to competitive adsorption by C3H8. Very similar trends were observed for C3H8; i.e. lower permeances were observed than during the binary mixture experiments and the permeance decreased with decreasing temperature as shown in Fig. 6. The maximum permeance observed for C3H8 during the ternary mixture separation experiments was 20×10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 323 K, and the permeance decreased to 8×10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 283 K, which was signi fi cantly lower than the permeance (49×10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 283 K) of C3H8 observed during binary mixture separation at the same temperature. This is most likely a result from the competitive adsorption and di ff usional coupling of n -C4H10 in the zeolite pores, with the tardier n -C4H10 slowing down the transport of C3H8. Consequently, in the ternary mixture, the C3H8/CH4 separation selectivities and permeance were only 9 and 15 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , respectively, at room temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As CH4 adsorbs much weaker than n -C4H10 and C3H8 in MFI zeolite, the adsorbed loading of CH4 will be low. Also, the CH4 transport is slowed down by the other adsorbed species due to correlation e ff ects. This may explain the low CH4 permanence observed during mixture separation. During the ternary mixture separation, the CH4 permeance varied from 3.2 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 to 0.5 × 10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 in the temperature range studied. Again, the highest separation selectivities of n -C4H10/CH4 and C3H8/CH4 were observed at the lowest temperature, albeit the values were lower than in the binary mixture viz. 32 and 15, respectively. As expected, the n -C4H10/CH4 selectivity increased faster than the C3H8/CH4 selectivity as the temperature was reduced, as a result of the higher heat of adsorption for n -C4H10 than for C3H8. In addition, a real natural gas feed will also contain heavier hydrocarbons which may adsorb strongly in the pores of MFI, perhaps leading to a slow loss of performance with time. However, the membrane can be regenerated by heating to e.g. 300 °C. In the present work, the membrane was heated to 300 °C before each separation experiment with a new gas mixture, indicating good thermal cycling stability. Further, the low thickness of the membrane with short di ff usion paths should be an advantage when regenerating. All these results indicate that ultra-thin MFI membranes are very promising for separation of C3+ from natural gas.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ultra-thin (400 nm) MFI zeolite membranes with high Si/Al ratio were used to separate n -C4H10 and C3H8 from binary and ternary mixtures with CH4 at di ff erent temperatures. The membranes showed good performance in terms of separation selectivity and permeance at all studied conditions. The C3+ separation selectivity was ascribed to adsorption selectivity and di ff usional coupling. For all separations, the separation selectivities increased and the permeance decreased with decreasing temperatures. The high permeance was attributed to the ultra-thin zeolite fi lms and open supports. The results show that ultrathin MFI zeolite membranes are very promising candidates for removal of C3+ hydrocarbons from natural gas.
texts.text: Ultra-thin (400 nm) MFI zeolite membranes with high Si/Al ratio were used to separate n -C4H10 and C3H8 from binary and ternary mixtures with CH4 at di ff erent temperatures. The membranes showed good performance in terms of separation selectivity and permeance at all studied conditions. The C3+ separation selectivity was ascribed to adsorption selectivity and di ff usional coupling. For all separations, the separation selectivities increased and the permeance decreased with decreasing temperatures. The high permeance was attributed to the ultra-thin zeolite fi lms and open supports. The results show that ultrathin MFI zeolite membranes are very promising candidates for removal of C3+ hydrocarbons from natural gas.
